entries.name: hello-world
entries.description: A tiny test skill. Writes a greeting into ~/.claude/skills/.
entries.author: launchpad-seed
entries.readme_md:
## What this skill does

`hello-world` is Launchpad's smallest end-to-end example — the skill equivalent of `print('hello')`. It ships one file (`SKILL.md`) and one pair of install commands that copy that file into `~/.claude/skills/hello-world/`.

It exists for two reasons:

1. **Smoke testing** the full run / install / verify flow without depending on any external repo.
2. **Teaching** the shape of a Reviewed-tier skill. The install commands use only closed-grammar verbs (`mkdir`, `cp`), so the pattern-analyzer + PR validator both accept it cleanly.

## What it installs

After `skillz install hello-world`, you'll have:

```
~/.claude/skills/hello-world/
├── SKILL.md         # a tiny prompt that Claude Code loads
└── .skillz-lock.json # generated by the installer; tracks the SHA
```

## Reading it as a template

Clone this and rename it as a starting point for your own skill:

```bash
skillz init my-first-skill
```

`skillz init` produces a Reviewed-eligible scaffold by default — no shell, no network, just files. That's the path of least resistance to a green **Reviewed** badge in the gallery.

## Verifying before install

Every skill's SHA is pinned in the registry. When you run:

```bash
skillz run hello-world
```

the CLI fetches the archive at that exact commit, shows you a two-panel diff of the files + install commands, and only executes when you type `yes`. Source is always one click away — `github.com/launchpad-skills/hello-world` — so you can read the whole thing in less than a minute.
